original_hypothesis.title: Withdrawal Request Starvation via Opportunistic Liquid Withdrawals
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: When the vault lacks sufficient liquid WRON to finalize an epoch, the operator must undelegate stake. However, once the undelegated RON enters the vault, it is immediately available to any user calling the standard ERC4626 `withdraw()` function. Opportunistic users can instantly drain the newly available liquid WRON before the operator can call `finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch()`. This creates a starvation condition where users who properly used `requestWithdrawal()` can never have their epochs finalized.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This breaks the withdrawal queue mechanism, allowing liquid withdrawers to front-cut queued users indefinitely.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw() does not reserve liquid WRON for pending withdrawal requests

preconditions: Vault has insufficient liquid WRON to finalize the current epoch
Operator undelegates stake to provide liquidity

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: withdraw() allows pulling up to IERC20(asset()).balanceOf(address(this))
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Liquid WRON intended for epoch finalization is unprotected
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Opportunistic users bypass the withdrawal queue and lock up queued users' funds

economic_questions: Can queued users be permanently starved of liquidity?
Does this force the operator to continuously undelegate, losing staking yield?
validation_requirements: Simulate undelegation followed by an immediate liquid withdrawal
Verify that finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch reverts due to insufficient WRON

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Time-value of money (immediate exit vs queued exit)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: High withdrawal demand exceeding liquid reserves

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users waiting in the withdrawal queue

validation_result.mechanism: Unprotected liquid WRON pool shared between instant withdrawals and epoch finalization
validation_result.impact_summary: Users who submitted withdrawal requests via requestWithdrawal() can be starved indefinitely because the liquid WRON needed for epoch finalization can be drained by other users calling the standard withdraw()/redeem() functions. There is no reservation mechanism.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The protocol provides two withdrawal paths: (1) instant ERC4626 withdraw()/redeem() that draws from liquid WRON in the vault, and (2) epoch-based requestWithdrawal() followed by operator-driven finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch(). When the vault lacks sufficient liquid WRON to finalize an epoch, the operator must undelegate stake via undelegateAmount(), which wraps the returned RON as WRON and deposits it into the vault. However, this newly available WRON is immediately accessible to any shareholder via withdraw() or redeem(). There is no maxWithdraw() override or reservation mechanism that accounts for the WRON needed to finalize pending withdrawal epochs. This means that between the operator's undelegation transaction and their finalization transaction, other users can drain all available WRON, causing finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() to revert on its SafeERC20.safeTransfer() call. This cycle can repeat indefinitely: operator undelegates -> users drain WRON -> finalization fails -> operator must undelegate more -> users drain again. Users who locked their shares via requestWithdrawal() have no cancellation mechanism and their shares are permanently locked in the contract.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The withdraw() function at LiquidRon.sol:258-266 calls super.withdraw() which ultimately calls _withdraw() which performs SafeERC20.safeTransfer() of WRON from vault to receiver. There is no check against any reserved amount for pending epochs. The finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() function at LiquidRon.sol:200-210 also calls _withdraw() which performs SafeERC20.safeTransfer() of WRON to the escrow. Both functions compete for the same liquid WRON pool. Neither maxWithdraw() nor maxRedeem() are overridden to account for lockedSharesPerEpoch.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No reservation of WRON for pending withdrawal epochs. Any user can drain all liquid WRON, preventing epoch finalization. maxWithdraw() is not overridden to subtract WRON needed for lockedSharesPerEpoch.
This function will revert if the vault's WRON balance is less than the assets value of lockedShares, which happens when other users have drained liquid WRON via withdraw()/redeem().
The safeTransfer will revert if the vault doesn't hold sufficient WRON. Both instant withdrawals and epoch finalization share this code path, competing for the same WRON pool.
Undelegated WRON is deposited directly into the vault's balance with no earmarking or reservation, making it immediately available for instant withdrawals.

calling_sequence: 1. Users call requestWithdrawal() (LiquidRon.sol:303-312) - shares transferred to vault, lockedSharesPerEpoch incremented
2. Vault has insufficient WRON to finalize, operator calls undelegateAmount() (LiquidRon.sol:189-193) -> LiquidProxy.undelegateAmount() wraps RON and sends WRON to vault
3. Between undelegation tx and finalization tx, User B calls withdraw() (LiquidRon.sol:258-266) -> super.withdraw() -> _withdraw() -> SafeERC20.safeTransfer() drains WRON from vault
4. Operator calls finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() (LiquidRon.sol:200-210) -> _withdraw() -> SafeERC20.safeTransfer(escrow, assets) REVERTS due to insufficient WRON balance
5. Queued users' shares remain locked in vault with no cancel mechanism
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The core issue is at withdraw() (line 258) which calls super.withdraw() which calls the overridden _withdraw() (line 350) that performs SafeERC20.safeTransfer(). The same _withdraw() function is also called by finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() (line 206). Both compete for IERC20(asset()).balanceOf(address(this)). There is no maxWithdraw() override to reserve WRON for lockedSharesPerEpoch. The standard ERC4626 maxWithdraw() only considers the owner's shares and totalAssets() - it does not subtract any reserved amounts. Similarly, redeem() at line 272 has the same issue.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script shows initial deposit of 10000 ether followed by delegation of all of it across validators. This confirms the scenario where most assets are staked and liquid WRON is low, making the starvation attack highly relevant.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario: (1) Vault has 50 WRON liquid and 950 staked. (2) User A calls requestWithdrawal(200 shares) worth ~200 WRON. Shares transfer to vault. (3) Operator calls undelegateAmount for 200 WRON worth of stake. Vault now has 250 WRON liquid. (4) User B front-runs finalization and calls withdraw(250 WRON). Transaction succeeds - vault has 0 WRON. (5) Operator's finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() reverts because _withdraw() tries SafeERC20.safeTransfer() of ~200 WRON from empty vault. (6) User A's shares are trapped with no cancel function. This can repeat indefinitely as long as there are users willing to make instant withdrawals. Even without malicious intent, normal user behavior creates this race condition.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. Users who called requestWithdrawal() can have their shares permanently locked if liquid withdrawals consistently drain available WRON before epoch finalization. There is no requestWithdrawal cancellation mechanism. The operator is forced into an arms race of repeatedly undelegating stake, losing staking yield, while liquid withdrawers keep draining the pool. This fundamentally breaks the withdrawal queue mechanism and could lead to permanent fund lockup for queued users.
recommended_mitigations: Override maxWithdraw() and maxRedeem() to subtract the WRON value of lockedSharesPerEpoch[currentEpoch] from available liquid WRON, preventing instant withdrawals from consuming reserved funds
Add a requestWithdrawalCancel() function allowing users to reclaim their locked shares if the epoch has not been finalized yet
Allow the operator to atomically undelegate and finalize in a single transaction, or provide a multi-call wrapper
Track a 'reservedWRON' state variable that is set when requestWithdrawal is called and checked in withdraw()/redeem() to prevent over-withdrawal
Consider having undelegateAmount send WRON directly to escrow when it's intended for epoch finalization
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LiquidRon with a mock staking contract. 2. Deposit 1000 WRON and delegate 900. 3. User A calls requestWithdrawal(200 shares). 4. Operator undelegates 200 WRON. 5. User B calls withdraw() for all available WRON. 6. Operator calls finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch() - expect revert. 7. Verify User A's shares are locked with no recourse.
uncertainties: The severity depends on the frequency of instant withdrawals vs queued withdrawals in practice
The operator could potentially use a custom contract to batch undelegation and finalization atomically, but this is not built into the protocol
The onlyOperator modifier bug (INV-ACCESS-001) may independently prevent finalization, making this issue secondary in current state
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test with concurrent instant and queued withdrawals to measure starvation frequency
Test whether the operator can call undelegateAmount and finaliseRonRewardsForEpoch atomically via a helper contract
Verify that the safeTransfer in _withdraw reverts cleanly when WRON balance is insufficient
